* Everything

 is having on the art of runway shows, especially the styling. Creative dir=
ectors Christophe Lemaire and Sarah-Linh Tran, who typically show a co-ed c=
ollection during Paris Men=E2=80=99s, have a way of twisting and arranging =
their garments and accessories into urban backpacking gear: tall boots for =
walking,
not hiking; crossbody-bags for the Metro, not the mountain; piled-on garmen=
ts for the streets, not the trail. This week in Milan, I noticed the Lemair=
e effect everywhere on the runway, from Max Mara=E2=80=94with all the long =
skirts and monochrome layering, and the cinched waists expertly belted by a=
ll-time great stylist Tonne Goodman=E2=80=94to Tod=E2=80=99s, whose show fe=
atured scrunchy turtlenecks, handkerchief skirts, and micro belt bags. Lema=
ire and Tran have released a way of being into the
ether.

Of course, Emporio Armani looked like nothing else. (I noted the sculpted, =
quilted jacket and the navy velvet suit piped in red and green.) If you lov=
e Armani, you almost always love it, and I do. Loro Piana, while very much =
its own thing, has certainly taken a styling cue or two from the Armani boo=
k, and is on a roll in an era when only the most luxurious are succeeding. =
Not only are the clothes getting more sumptuous, with more satin and boucle=
 tweeds, but its
accessories are also among the most desirable on the market: The high-throa=
t pumps in various materials, colors, heel heights, and shapes are going st=
rong. (There were 75 women=E2=80=99s looks in total.)

=3DLine+Sheet+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F28%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm=

in person: The twisty velvet heeled sandals looked so easy, which helps to =

Hanbury, has quickly become such a staple for so many. Industry vet Paul An=
drew=E2=80=99s debut at Lanvin Group=E2=80=93owned Sergio Rossi was dynamit=
e. The collection came together incredibly quickly, which Andrew credits to=
 the marvel that is the Sergio Rossi factory, but is really the result of h=
is ingenuity combined with years of experience across brands. (I doubt ther=
e=E2=80=99s a factory in Italy that Andrew doesn=E2=80=99t know like the ba=
ck of his hand.) It felt so new, from the way he sliced
open the toe of the tango shoe, to the Mongolian-fur-covered boots that=E2=
=80=99ll soon become paparazzi fixtures.

In other news, MM6 was looking very Martina Tiefenthaler=E2=80=93influenced=
, styled in the vein of Demna, her longtime boss. (She=E2=80=99s been worki=
ng in the OTB-owned studio for some time.) New Maison Margiela creative dir=
ector Glenn Martens sat front row, and I wondered whether Martens would be =
more involved in the secondary line than his
predecessor, John Galliano, ever was. Parentco OTB does a great job with MM=
6 and Margiela=E2=80=99s more commercial endeavors, like shoes. The recent =
collaboration with Christian Louboutin was pretty genius, and the coats in =
this show were great, too.

I want to like Moschino as much as I like Adrian Appiolaza=E2=80=99s clever=
 ideas=E2=80=94an =E2=80=9CM=E2=80=9D as a back belt loop on a pair of trou=
sers instead of an =E2=80=9CX,=E2=80=9D or his cut-out collars. But it feel=
s as if Appiolaza is stuck
between those little flourishes=E2=80=94which can presumably be produced en=
 masse for commercial purposes=E2=80=94and something more serious that he d=
oesn=E2=80=99t seem ready, or able, to express. Meanwhile, the vision is cl=
ear at Sunnei, which you might want to call the Eckhlaus Latta of Milan. (D=

al.) I loved the color-blocking and velvety jeans cut open up the back of t=
he calf. It was also incredibly clever

Versace was Friday=E2=80=99s most consequential show. The fate of the busin=
ess is unknown; the fate of creative director Donatella Versace is unknown.=
 (Will she stay? Will she go? Will she work alongside Dario Vitale if he is=

n show they=E2=80=99ve ever seen; another person I talked to said it was ev=
erything she wants in a fashion show. (Her only complaint was that Eminem=

Personally, I had a great time: I love a raised catwalk, the benches were h=
eated
(we were outside, at night, in a train station), and the boom boom clothes=
=E2=80=94to steal the newly coined phrase for our current obsession with =
=E2=80=9980s opulence=E2=80=94were closer to what Versace should be than an=
ything she=E2=80=99s shown in a while. Give me a velvet mini dress and a sc=
arf-printed blouse anytime, and in return, give her a bit of resolution on =
this ownership thing.

* Of double-dip tariffs and pricing pencils: President Trump=E2=80=99s tari=
ff tsunami is predicted to hit again on March 4, when imports from Canada a=
nd Mexico

 with China seeing tariff stacking just one month after the initial hit, an=

in:0 0 24px 0;"><em>Everything
Lauren saw in the past 48 hours in Milan</em>. <em>Okay, not everything but=
=E2=80=A6</em>: We can no longer ignore the effect that Lemaire=E2=80=99s c=
atwalk prowess is having on the art of runway shows, especially the styling=
=2E Creative directors <strong>Christop=
he</strong> <strong>Lemaire</strong> a=
nd <strong>Sarah-Linh Tran</strong>, who typically show a co-ed collection =
during Paris Men=E2=80=99s, have a way of twisting and arranging their garm=
ents and accessories into urban backpacking gear: tall boots for walking,
not hiking; crossbody-bags for the Metro, not the mountain; piled-on garmen=
ts for the streets, not the trail. This week in Milan, I noticed the Lemair=
e effect everywhere on the runway, from Max Mara=E2=80=94with all the long =
skirts and monochrome layering, and the cinched waists expertly belted by a=
ll-time great stylist <strong>Tonne Goodman</strong>=E2=80=94to Tod=E2=80=
=99s, whose show featured scrunchy turtlenecks, handkerchief skirts, and mi=
cro belt bags. Lemaire and Tran have released a way of being into the
ether.<br/><br/>Of course, Emporio Armani looked like nothing else. (I note=
d the sculpted, quilted jacket and the navy velvet suit piped in red and gr=
een.) If you love Armani, you almost <em>always</em> love it, and I do. Lor=
o Piana, while very much its own thing, has certainly taken a styling cue o=
r two from the Armani book, and is on a roll in an era when only the most l=
uxurious are succeeding. Not only are the clothes getting more sumptuous, w=
ith more satin and boucle tweeds, but its
accessories are also among the most desirable on the market: The high-throa=
t pumps in various materials, colors, heel heights, and shapes are going st=
rong. (There were<em> 75</em> women=E2=80=99s looks in total.)<br/><br/>On =
the shoes beat, I loved seeing <a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlb=
WFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dWVHE1VXJuVlhIazZDOUFtRzl3dz0iLCJocmVm=
IjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9mYXZlLmNvLzNEb0VXam4_dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPUxpbmUrU2hlZXQrLStTVUJ=
TQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgyJTJGMjglMkYyNSUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUxpbmUrU2hlZXQrLS=
tTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgyJTJGMjglMkYyNSUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWxfYWN0a=
W9uXHUwMDI2dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1jdXN0b21lci5pb1x1MDAyNnV0bV90ZXJtPWY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0=
MmFiZDEyYSIsImludGVybmFsIjoiZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwibGlua19pZCI6MjA5NzM=
2M30/e61ef64b4ebd884cbd9657c41f90d9a8e560b39c0b44c45ad520946544bf8810" styl=
e=3D"text-decoration:underline;color:#d40000;">Le Monde B=C3=A9ryl</a>=E2=
=80=99s full range in person: The twisty velvet heeled sandals looked so ea=
sy, which helps to explain why the line, designed by <strong>Lily Atherton
Hanbury</strong>, has quickly become such a staple for so many. Industry ve=
t <strong>Paul Andrew</strong>=E2=80=99s debut at Lanvin Group=E2=80=93owne=
d Sergio Rossi was dynamite. The collection came together incredibly quickl=
y, which Andrew credits to the marvel that is the Sergio Rossi factory, but=
 is really the result of his ingenuity combined with years of experience ac=
ross brands. (I doubt there=E2=80=99s a factory in Italy that Andrew doesn=
=E2=80=99t know like the back of his hand.) It felt so new, from the way he=
 sliced
open the toe of the tango shoe, to the Mongolian-fur-covered boots that=E2=
=80=99ll soon become paparazzi fixtures.<br/><br/>In other news, MM6 was lo=
oking very <strong>Martina Tiefenthaler</strong>=E2=80=93influenced, styled=
 in the vein of <strong>Demna</strong>, her longtime boss. (She=E2=80=99s b=
een working in the OTB-owned studio for some time.) New Maison Margiela cre=
ative director <strong>Glenn Martens</strong> sat front row, and I wondered=
 whether Martens would be more involved in the secondary line than his
predecessor, <strong>John Galliano</strong>, ever was. Parentco OTB does a =
great job with MM6 and Margiela=E2=80=99s more commercial endeavors, like s=
hoes. The recent collaboration with Christian Louboutin was pretty genius, =
and the coats in this show were great, too.<br/><br/>I <em>want</em> to lik=
e Moschino as much as I like <strong>Adrian Appiolaza</strong>=E2=80=99s cl=
ever ideas=E2=80=94an =E2=80=9CM=E2=80=9D as a back belt loop on a pair of =
trousers instead of an =E2=80=9CX,=E2=80=9D or his cut-out collars. But it =
feels as if Appiolaza is stuck
between those little flourishes=E2=80=94which can presumably be produced en=
 masse for commercial purposes=E2=80=94and something more serious that he d=
oesn=E2=80=99t seem ready, or able, to express. Meanwhile, the vision is cl=
ear at Sunnei, which you might want to call the Eckhlaus Latta of Milan. (D=
esigners <strong>Simone Rizzo</strong> and <strong>Loris Messina</strong> m=
anage to be both cool and commercial.) I loved the color-blocking and velve=
ty jeans cut open up the back of the calf. It was also incredibly clever
to have the audience walk through a little retail store on the way out. (It=
 reminded me of Eckhaus Latta=E2=80=99s Chinatown shop in Los Angeles.)<br/=
><br/>Versace was Friday=E2=80=99s most consequential show. The fate of the=
 business is unknown; the fate of creative director <strong>Donatella Versa=
ce</strong> is unknown. (Will she stay? Will she go? Will she work alongsid=
e <strong>Dario Vitale </strong>if he is indeed joining?) All I know is tha=
t Versace went for it on Friday night in a display of opulence
reminiscent of the brand=E2=80=99s glory days, if not as potent. Whatever i=
t was, it was divisive. One person I talked to said it was the worst fashio=
n show they=E2=80=99ve ever seen; another person I talked to said it was ev=
erything she wants in a fashion show. (Her only complaint was that <strong>=
Eminem</strong>=E2=80=94whose song =E2=80=9CMy Name Is=E2=80=9D was sampled=
, with =E2=80=9CVersace=E2=80=9D replacing =E2=80=9CSlim Shady=E2=80=9D=E2=
=80=94did not come out to perform live.)<br/><br/>Personally, I had a great=
 time: I love a raised catwalk, the benches were heated
(we were outside, at night, in a train station), and the boom boom clothes=
=E2=80=94to steal the newly coined phrase for our current obsession with =
=E2=80=9980s opulence=E2=80=94were closer to what Versace should be than an=
ything she=E2=80=99s shown in a while. Give me a velvet mini dress and a sc=
arf-printed blouse anytime, and in return, give her a bit of resolution on =
this ownership thing.</li><li style=3D"Margin:0 0 24px 0;"><em>Rothy=E2=80=
=99s Bon March=C3=A9-ing into Paris</em>:<em>
</em><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt=

ation:underline;color:#d40000;">Rothy=E2=80=99s</a> debut at Le Bon March=

g its sustainable, washable footwear alongside more upscale fashion at one =
of the world=E2=80=99s most prestigious and discerning department stores. I=

 reported more than $200 million in revenue and double-digit YoY growth whi=
le achieving
profitability and double-digit EBITDA margins. As I was reminded by an anal=
yst who knows retail inside and out, hitting $300 million demonstrates that=
 a brand is ready for the big leagues. And while Rothy=E2=80=99s isn=E2=80=
=99t quite there yet, it=E2=80=99s on the cusp.<br/><br/>Rothy=E2=80=99s ha=
s already passed the upmarket trial run at Liberty of London, where it bega=
n as a pop-up and evolved into a permanent space. Stateside, the brand is e=
xpanding its wholesale footprint by opening in 20 Nordstrom locations. Now,=
 in Paris,
Rothy=E2=80=99s has to prove it can sell its ballet flats (and other wares)=
 in the ballet flat capital of the world.=C2=A0<br/><br/>Lucky for them, th=
e Le Bon March=C3=A9 shop isn=E2=80=99t just =E2=80=9Cshoes on a table,=E2=
=80=9D but a dedicated space showcasing the brand=E2=80=99s story through i=
nstallations. This treatment is a huge vote of confidence=E2=80=94L.B.M., w=
hich is particular about which brands get to use special fixtures, is expla=
ining to a predominantly new audience why these shoes are worth buying.<br/=
><br/>Meanwhile, more L.A. brands

th Le Patio opening on Floor 1 of Le Bon March=C3=A9, brands like D=C3=B4en=

rtment (and therefore more open-to-buy dollars). <em>=E2=80=94Sarah Shapiro=
</em></li><li style=3D"Margin:0 0 24px 0;"><em>Of double-dip tariffs and pr=
icing pencils</em>: President<strong> Trump</strong>=E2=80=99s tariff tsuna=
mi is predicted to hit again on March 4, when imports from Canada and Mexic=
o
are scheduled to face a hefty 25 percent levy, while China gets an extra 10=
 percent bonus tariff on top of the 10 percent that was introduced earlier =
this month. As always, take these threats with a grain of salt: Trump pause=
d the tariffs on Mexico and Canada earlier this month, and he could do so a=
gain. He vowed this week to slap a 25 percent tariff on the E.U., but that =
could simply be an opening bid in a more protracted negotiation, too.<br/><=
br/>Whatever happens, all this uncertainty is giving
retailers pricing-strategy <em>d=C3=A9j=C3=A0 vu,</em> as they must again d=
ecide whether to absorb higher costs, or pass them on to shoppers. Many bra=

ian and Mexican import tariffs by moving extra merchandise into U.S. wareho=
